architecture_drivers.driver: Public contribution checkout and payout must use an external provider boundary.
Trip ledger, private event ledger, and contribution references must remain separate.
Receipt source values must be preserved separately from OCR, translation, and display FX.
Public/private visibility and moderation boundaries are first-class architecture concerns.

architecture_decisions.decision: Use one member-facing web app plus an admin operations surface backed by modular backend domains.
Model public contributions through an ExternalContributionProvider boundary instead of Crewly-owned payment processing.
Run OCR, translation, FX lookup, notifications, and provider reconciliation asynchronously.
Use modular monolith service boundaries for MVP with explicit domain ownership.
Keep Contribution Reference separate from Ledger.
Use a relational primary data model with scoped ownership and append-only audit events.
Store original receipt values separately from OCR, translation, and display currency values.
Expose role-scoped application APIs and keep provider/admin APIs internal.
Use provider adapters for all external services.
Provider failures degrade to pending/manual states rather than blocking core trip access.
Use role and scope based authorization across all private resources.
Use a standard web app, relational database, object storage, queue, and worker deployment model.
Scale expensive and failure-prone workflows independently through queues and workers.
Optimize first for correctness, scope separation, and auditability before high-scale collaboration.

architecture_risks.risk: External provider selection may limit contribution status import or reconciliation.
architecture_risks.mitigation: Design provider-agnostic references with manual reconciliation fallback.

architecture_risks.risk: Improper API authorization could expose private trip or event data.
architecture_risks.mitigation: Centralize access checks and require scope checks for every private resource.

architecture_risks.risk: Visibility bugs could leak private trip, event, receipt, or media data.
architecture_risks.mitigation: Centralize visibility policy and test public/private/event participant transitions.

architecture_risks.risk: AI/document-processing costs may grow faster than monetization.
architecture_risks.mitigation: Add usage limits, on-demand processing, and cost telemetry before launch.

tradeoffs.benefit: faster MVP development and simpler transactions
tradeoffs.cost: requires discipline to preserve service boundaries

tradeoffs.choice: external contribution provider over direct payments
tradeoffs.benefit: mitigates payment compliance risk for MVP
tradeoffs.cost: provider UX, export, and sync limitations

tradeoffs.choice: async processing for receipts and integrations

open_questions.question: Which external contribution provider will be selected for implementation?

answered_questions: Stage 2 selected external-provider contribution handling for MVP.
assumptions_made: MVP uses a modular monolith with explicit service/domain boundaries.
Receipt OCR, translation, FX display, notifications, and external provider sync run asynchronously.
Concrete provider choices are deferred to Stage 5 unless they change architecture boundaries.
Admin operations can be a separate protected surface within the same application platform.
user_confirmations: Use Option 3 for contribution handling: external provider owns checkout, KYC, refunds, disputes, and payout.
architecture_confidence_gaps: Detailed cost model for OCR, translation, storage, and provider sync.

ux_architecture_inputs: public trip page must show external-provider contribution CTA
private trip workspace requires member access state
private event workspace requires participant-scoped access
ledger summaries must separate contribution references, trip expenses, and private event expenses
receipt views must distinguish original values from OCR/translation/display FX
async processing states are required for receipts, contribution sync, and notifications

completion_status.reason: Stage 3 architecture outputs are complete enough for UX and interaction architecture.
next_actions: Begin Stage 4 UX and interaction architecture.

development_orchestration_inputs: service IDs should drive implementation slices
database schema must enforce ledger scope separation
provider selection is required before external contribution automation
authorization and audit tests are mandatory for sensitive workflows
async worker infrastructure must precede receipt/provider processing tickets

completion_status.reason: Architecture is usable for later development orchestration after Stage 4 UX architecture defines screens and interaction behavior.
next_actions: Use Stage 4 outputs plus this architecture package for Stage 5 planning.

completion_status.reason: All eight Stage 3 architecture outputs exist, service/data/API/integration/security/infrastructure/scalability foundations are defined, and high-risk items have mitigation paths.
next_actions: Run Stage 4 UX and interaction architecture using the Stage 4 handoff.
Carry Stage 5 provider selection and cost model gaps forward without blocking Stage 4.
